AI Newspeak: Why Your Agent Can’t Explain ItselfArtificial Intelligence

Knowledge Graph

13 min read
Just now

--

If you’re using AI agents for high-stakes decisions right now — denying loans, flagging fraud, prioritizing major projects — you’re taking a risk. But when you’re ready to do it right, this article offers an approach.
Can your AI agent explain exactly why it reached a conclusion?
Not a narrative explanation. Not “the model considered various factors.” I mean a formal, auditable chain of reasoning that a regulator, auditor, or opposing counsel can trace step by step.
If you’re using any of the popular agentic frameworks — AutoGPT, CrewAI, LangGraph, or even custom ReAct implementations — the answer is no. You can’t. These systems produce narrative reasoning: plausible-sounding chains of text that read like explanations but can’t be formally verified. Call it Newspeak for the AI age — language designed to sound like thinking.
When Claude or GPT-4 “plans” a multi-step task, you get something like:
“I’ll first query the customer table, then join with invoices, then filter for VIP status, then calculate the quarterly trend…”
This looks like reasoning. It feels like reasoning. But there’s no separation between the logical structure of the proof, the facts required to support it, and the inference rules connecting them. The reasoning and the justification are the same blob of text, generated after the fact to sound convincing.
Even commercial systems designed for multi-step reasoning often just break the problem into steps and produce similar language. The steps are organized, but the provenance is still narrative. You get a cleaner workflow, not an auditable proof. Some do better — generating deterministic code that can be inspected and verified. That’s one step removed from a true proof: you can see what the code does, but not why it was the right code to run, or how the conclusion traces back to source facts. Helpful, but not quite what I was looking for.
This points to two distinct modes of AI reasoning. Exploratory mode — sequential steps in a DAG, where the agent plans, executes, and adjusts. Provenance exists, but it’s looser; you know what happened, but the “why” is narrative. That’s fine for research, discovery, and brainstorming. Then there’s auditable mode — strict depth-first fact resolution where every node in the proof tree has explicit provenance, confidence scores, and source attribution. The proof either holds or it fails, and you can see exactly where. For critical decisioning — where conclusions must be explainable, auditable, and defensible — only the second mode survives scrutiny.
What We Knew in 1984
The irony is that we solved this problem four decades ago. In 1984 — the year Orwell’s dystopia was finally set — expert systems were at their peak, and they got reasoning right. While the Party’s Newspeak was designed to make truth unverifiable, MYCIN did the opposite: every conclusion came with a traceable proof. In the enterprise AI age, “narrative reasoning” plays the same role Newspeak did — it creates a world where the answer is whatever the model says it is, and the “why” is rewritten after the fact.
MYCIN, the famous medical diagnosis system from Stanford, could tell you exactly why it concluded a patient had a bacterial infection: “Symptom A (confidence 0.8) AND lab result B (confidence 0.9) triggered rule R17.” You could audit the entire chain, challenge any fact, and see exactly how confidence propagated through the system.
This wasn’t magic. It was backward chaining — a reasoning strategy where you start with the conclusion you’re trying to prove and recursively decompose it into sub-goals until you reach facts you can verify.
PROVE: Patient has bacterial infection
REQUIRES: Gram stain is positive
FACT: Lab result [verified]
REQUIRES: Patient has fever > 101F
FACT: Vital signs [verified]
REQUIRES: No viral markers present
FACT: Blood panel [verified]Every node in this tree has a clear provenance. You know where each fact came from, how confident you are in it, and exactly how it contributes to the conclusion. When the proof fails, you can see precisely where it broke.
The expert systems era eventually collapsed under its own weight — the “knowledge acquisition bottleneck” made it impossibly expensive to manually encode all the rules needed for complex domains. But the core insight about auditable reasoning was sound. We just forgot it in the rush toward statistical learning.
I’ve seen what happens when complex organizations lose the thread of their own logic. Whether it’s the financial collapses of the early 2000s or the regulatory scrutiny of the EU AI Act today, the cost of “we can’t explain this” is always higher than the cost of building it right.
I didn’t forget. I built two of them: a corporate tax planning system for a large consulting firm and a hearing loss diagnostic system that ran in production for over a decade. I observed expert systems die due to the knowledge acquisition bottleneck.
When LLMs began to demonstrate real reasoning capabilities, I wondered whether I could integrate my old expert system ideas with this new technology. Wrap them in Python, get the best of both worlds? The answer turned out to be yes — but not the way the neuro-symbolic crowd is doing it. They got the architecture right, then buried it under enterprise infrastructure.
Could I have built backward chaining in pure Python? Probably. But I stumbled on ProbLog — a lightweight probabilistic logic library — and it already solved the hard parts: recursive proof search, confidence propagation, the fiddly bits I didn’t want to reimplement. Why reinvent when you can pip install?
So I built it. Why? Because in my mind, anything can be solved — coding is just work. I have boundless (and perhaps unfounded) optimism around my ability to figure things out. Seventy hours later, I had a working system.
I used Claude Code extensively to build it. I’m still not convinced I could have hand-coded it just as quickly — but somehow AI-assisted coding has become my new default. I’ve had a range of good and bad experiences with vibe coding (and I’m training myself as much as the tools). But I noticed something: this kind of highly structured reasoning — proof trees, fact resolution chains, explicit dependencies — worked remarkably well with Claude Code. The same discipline that makes proofs auditable seems to make the code easier to generate.
Here’s the thing: the LLM is the solution to that bottleneck. It understands context, infers rules, and knows what “VIP customer” probably means in your domain. That’s brilliant. The LLM still needs schema, definitions, relationships — it can’t infer proofs about data it doesn’t understand. But what it doesn’t need is heavy external runtimes — standalone Prolog servers, OWL reasoners, enterprise inference platforms. Lightweight symbolic libraries, such as ProbLog, provide backward chaining and confidence propagation without the overhead of infrastructure. Maybe at massive scale — moon launch complexity — a dedicated symbolic platform earns its keep. But 99% of enterprise decisions don’t need that. They need auditability, not exotic infrastructure.
The LLM Insight Everyone’s Missing
Here’s what most people get wrong about LLMs and reasoning: they treat the model as the reasoner. Ask it a question, get a chain-of-thought response, trust the narrative.
The breakthrough isn’t a new model or framework. It’s prompt engineering. Instead of asking the LLM to answer, ask it to prove. Instead of “Is this customer a VIP?”, ask “What would need to be true for this customer to be a VIP?” The LLM doesn’t change. The framing does.
That shift — from answering to proving — changes everything. Now the LLM generates a proof structure: the shape of what would need to be true to support a conclusion. Then, deterministic systems acquire the facts and evaluate the logic.
The LLM is brilliant at understanding what “VIP customer” might mean in context, what tables likely contain revenue data, and how business rules typically work. It’s terrible at consistently applying those rules or knowing what it doesn’t know.
Here’s another thing I discovered: LLMs generate fact-resolution code remarkably well. Given a schema and a fact to resolve, they reliably produce the SQL query, API call, or data transformation needed to fetch it. The code is deterministic and verifiable — even if the LLM that wrote it isn’t.
I also added an automated learning loop. When code generation fails but ultimately succeeds after retries, I capture those cases — what went wrong, what fixed it. These codegen insights are summarized and incorporated into the prompts. No fine-tuning, no retraining — just accumulated wisdom in context. It dramatically improved retry success rates.
So give it the jobs it’s good at: generating proof structures and writing the code to resolve facts. The only thing it can’t do is know your data. That’s where the fact resolution service comes in — and honestly, it’s not that hard. A disciplined lookup chain that checks cache, config, databases, and asks the user when it can’t find something. No knowledge graphs. No heavyweight infrastructure. Just a service that answers “what is X?” with provenance attached.
LLM generates:
{
"assertion": "Customer C001 is a VIP",
"requires": [
{"fact": "customer_revenue", "args": ["C001"], "compare": ">", "against": "vip_threshold"},
{"fact": "vip_threshold"}
]
}System executes:
RESOLVE customer_revenue(C001) -> $150,000 [from: DATABASE, confidence: 1.0]
RESOLVE vip_threshold -> $100,000 [from: CONFIG, confidence: 1.0]
EVALUATE: 150,000 > 100,000 -> TRUEPROVED with full audit trailThe proof either holds or it doesn’t. And you can see exactly where it came from.
Recent academic work validates this approach. Google’s LAMBADA paper (2022) showed that backward chaining with LLMs dramatically outperforms forward reasoning on problems requiring deep proof chains. A 2024 paper on Symbolic Backward Chaining went further: let a symbolic solver control the entire process, calling the LLM only when the solver hits a dead end. The results showed significant improvements in accuracy, proof faithfulness, and efficiency.
LLMs are better at generating proof structures than executing them.
The Fact Resolution Chain
Before diving into the mechanics, it’s worth being explicit: not every AI task needs this level of rigor. If you’re exploring a dataset, brainstorming product ideas, or researching a market — exploratory reasoning is fine. The agent walks you through the steps; you see what it tried, and the narrative explanation is good enough. But the moment someone asks, “Why did you deny this loan?” or “Show me how you flagged this transaction,” you’ve crossed into auditable territory. The fact resolution chain is the machinery that enables auditable reasoning.
In practice, I found myself using both. Exploratory mode is faster for iteration — the steps are larger, more composite, which frustrates fine-grained provenance but gets you to a provisional answer quickly. Then I switch to auditable mode to generate the formal proof. Speed when you’re figuring it out, rigor when it matters.
The practical heart of this architecture is a disciplined hierarchy for resolving facts. When the proof requires a fact, the system walks down this chain until it finds an answer:

• Cache: Already proved this session.

• Config: Explicit business rules and thresholds.

• Data Sources: Ground truth from DBs/APIs.

• Derived: Composite facts requiring sub-proofs.

• LLM Foundational: General training knowledge.

• LLM Researched: Real-time web search/tool use.

• User: Explicit handoff for missing information.
Each level carries a confidence score. Cache inherits from the original proof. Config and Data Sources get 1.0 — ground truth. Derived facts get the minimum of their dependencies. LLM knowledge gets discounted (0.6
